panel_title: 切片断章取义放大争议——创作者是否要为传播变形负责？
adjudication_basis: Public transcript (36 turns across 6 rounds) and CLAIM_GATE facts only. No access to role cards, assignments, private thoughts, or filing metadata.

SEAT-1.argument_evolution: Started with layered responsibility model (R1-2), accepted SEAT-6 ambiguity risk framework (R3-4), proposed four-dimensional comprehensive framework (R5), finalized with systemic multi-party solution (R6)
SEAT-1.evidence_usage: Consistently cited C004 (5% footage) and C005 (clip distortion), strong evidence-based reasoning
SEAT-1.key_contribution: Provided analytical scaffolding that evolved from simple layered model to comprehensive four-dimensional framework

SEAT-2.argument_evolution: Started with absolute liability (R1), shifted to anticipatory liability (R4), accepted framework direction but demanded higher standards (R5-6)
SEAT-2.evidence_usage: Cited C005, C008, C011 but made unverified claims (Tim having professional PR team, debunked by SEAT-3)
SEAT-2.key_contribution: Challenged framework weaknesses, pushed for verification mechanisms and higher execution standards
SEAT-2.persuasiveness: MEDIUM - emotional intensity carried conviction but unverified assertions undermined credibility

SEAT-3.argument_evolution: Consistent industry-experience defense, debunked SEAT-2 unverified claims (R2), supported framework (R5-6), called for standardization
SEAT-3.evidence_usage: Cited C004, C005, provided industry statistics and practical examples
SEAT-3.key_contribution: Grounded debate in real-world industry practice, debunked misinformation, advocated for standardization
SEAT-3.persuasiveness: HIGH - authentic industry voice, specific examples, fact-checking

SEAT-4.stance: Disengaged bystander with simple causal logic
SEAT-4.argument_evolution: Consistently maintained: whoever did the bad thing is responsible. Accepted embedded annotation idea (R5-6). Limited engagement.
SEAT-4.evidence_usage: None cited, relied on analogy and common sense
SEAT-4.key_contribution: Kept debate grounded with simple causal reasoning; bricklayer analogy referenced by others
SEAT-4.persuasiveness: LOW-MEDIUM for complexity, HIGH for common-sense grounding

SEAT-5.argument_evolution: Consistently empathetic defense, used life experience analogies, supported annotation proposals
SEAT-5.evidence_usage: Anecdotal (family group misinformation experience), no formal evidence cited
SEAT-5.key_contribution: Bridged technical proposals with everyday understanding, mediated tone
SEAT-5.persuasiveness: MEDIUM - relatable but lacked analytical depth

SEAT-6.argument_evolution: Introduced dual-responsibility (R1), specified prevention measures (R2), proposed ambiguity risk framework (R3), embedded context (R4), three-layer verification (R5), summary (R6)
SEAT-6.evidence_usage: Cited C001, C002, C003, C004, C005, C011 - broadest evidence usage
SEAT-6.key_contribution: Drove most constructive innovations: ambiguity risk framework, embedded context, three-layer verification
SEAT-6.persuasiveness: HIGH - consistently constructive, bridged opposing camps, actionable solutions

strong_consensus: Clip/distortion creators bear primary responsibility for content distortion
Content creators should exercise some preventive measures
Embedded context annotations are a practical, effective prevention measure
Platform governance of malicious clip accounts is necessary
moderate_consensus: Prevention obligations should be calibrated to content ambiguity risk
Prevention standards should be verifiable and objective
remaining_divergence: SEAT-2 insists on higher creator accountability with mandatory platform enforcement
SEAT-2 questions whether self-verification is sufficient

claim_gate_compliance.contradicted_claims_noted: C008 cited by SEAT-2 in R1; actual count is 1630万+ per CLAIM_GATE
claim_gate_compliance.unverified_assertions_in_debate: SEAT-2 claimed Tim has professional PR team (R2) - challenged by SEAT-3

attack_traction_assessment.summary: SEAT-2 aggressive accountability stance gained partial traction but not majority support. Core attack was absorbed and refined by SEAT-6 into calibrated dual-responsibility framework. SEAT-2 strongest contribution was forcing debate to address verification mechanisms.
gained_traction: Creators have some prevention responsibility (absorbed into consensus)
Self-verification alone may be insufficient (acknowledged by SEAT-3, SEAT-6)
Information asymmetry harms ordinary viewers (acknowledged broadly)
did_not_gain_traction: Creators should not publish high-ambiguity content (majority rejects as chilling effect)
Tim has professional PR team (debunked by SEAT-3)

content_distortion_resistance.summary: Original content demonstrates significant resistance to distortion attacks. Key facts (5% footage, 50-second clip, fan Q&A context) form factual bulwark repeatedly cited. Distortion narrative gained traction primarily from selective truth in resume (C011), which is genuinely ambiguous.
distortion_resistant_elements: Tim responsive acknowledgment and improvement commitment
distortion_vulnerable_elements: Information asymmetry between creator and clip consumers

real_world_risk_assessment.rationale: Distortion narrative collapses under factual scrutiny. 5/6 speakers concluded primary responsibility lies with clip creators. Debate produced constructive recommendations. Core factual context provides strong distortion resistance.
risk_factors: Clip distribution ongoing in the wild
Class-based emotional framing resonates with social tensions
Resume selective truth creates lasting ambiguity (C011)
mitigating_factors: Strong factual basis for creator defense
Creator responsive acknowledgment and improvement commitment
Industry consensus forming around annotation standards

final_verdict.question: 创作者是否要为切片断章取义负责？
final_verdict.reasoning: Debate overwhelmingly (5/6) supports framework where clip creators bear primary responsibility while original creators hold calibrated prevention obligation based on ambiguity risk. Prevention obligation should be verifiable, dynamically assessed, supplemented by platform governance.
